1: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe bemidbar Sinai be'ohel mo'ed: 'YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tent of meeting.' The book of Numbers opens at the intersection of three theological locations: midbar (wilderness — the in-between place, neither Egypt nor the land), Sinai (the mountain of covenant), and ohel mo'ed (the tent of meeting — the completed tabernacle from which YHWH now speaks, Exodus 40:34-35). The divine speech originates from the tabernacle's interior: YHWH who filled the tent with his glory now issues orders from within it. 'The first day of the second month in the second year' places this one month after the tabernacle's completion (Exodus 40:17).
2: seu et rosh: 'lift the head of' — the Hebrew idiom for conducting a census. Nasa (lift) + rosh (head) = the counting metaphor: each person's head is lifted/registered. The book's Hebrew name Bemidbar (in the wilderness) is preferred over the Greek 'Numbers' (arithmoi) derived from this census. The count proceeds lemishpechotam lebeit avotam (by their clans, by their fathers' houses) — two levels of social organization: mishpacha (extended family/clan) and beit av (father's house, the nuclear family unit). Legulgelotam: by their heads/skulls (from gulgolet = skull; the same root as Golgotha, 'place of the skull').
3: kol yotzei tzava be-Yisrael: 'all who go out to war in Israel' — the military framing of the census. Yotzei tzava (those who go out of the host/army) defines the counted group: adult males 20 years and older capable of military service. Tzava (host/army) is also the word behind YHWH tzvaot (LORD of hosts) — the cosmic military title. Israel's earthly army reflects the divine heavenly host. Tifkedu otam le-tzivotam: 'you shall enroll them by their companies/armies' — the corporate military unit (tzava) is the organizing principle.
4: ve'itchem yihyu ish ish lamatteh: 'with you shall be a man from each tribe' — one leader per tribe. Ish ish (each man / a man, a man) is the emphatic distributive construction stressing individual responsibility. Matteh (staff/tribe) — the tribe takes its name from the leader's staff; matteh means both 'rod/staff' (the leader's symbol) and 'tribe' (those grouped under that staff). The dual meaning preserves the organic connection between leader and community.
5: ve'eleh shemot ha-anashim asher ya'amdu itchem: 'and these are the names of the men who shall assist you.' Ya'amdu (shall stand/assist) — the verb amad (stand) expresses readiness and appointment; standing before someone = serving them. The leader 'stands with' Moses and Aaron in the census-taking. Lir'uven (for Reuben): Elitzur ben Shedeur — 'my God is a rock, son of the shedder of light.' The names are theophoric; each leader's name reflects covenant theology.
6: lishim'on: for Simeon — Shelumi'el ben Tzurishaddai: 'my peace is God, son of the rock of Shaddai.' Shaddai (the Almighty/El Shaddai) appears in the father's name; the Shaddai title, prominent in Genesis, continues through the tribal lineage.
7: liYhudah: for Judah — Nachshon ben Aminadav: 'serpent (or diver), son of the willing people.' Nachshon ben Aminadav will later be identified in rabbinic tradition as the first to step into the Red Sea. His name appears in the genealogy of David (Ruth 4:20) and in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:4). The Judahite leader stands in the direct ancestral line of the Messiah.
8: liYissachar: for Issachar — Netanel ben Tzuar: 'God has given, son of the rock.' The gift-name (natan el) reflects the covenant blessing. Issachar's tribe will later be noted for 'understanding of the times' (1 Chronicles 12:32).
9: liZevulun: for Zebulun — Eli'av ben Chelon: 'my God is father, son of strength.' Av (father) in the leader's name reflects the patriarchal theology: God as father of Israel. Zebulun's territory will later encompass the Galilee region where Jesus began his ministry (Matthew 4:13-16, citing Isaiah 9:1-2).
10: livnei Yosef: 'for the sons of Joseph' — Joseph's double-tribe inheritance (Ephraim and Manasseh) is listed under his name as the parent tribe. le-Efrayim: Elishama ben Ammihud: 'my God has heard, son of my people is glorious.' liMenashe: Gamliel ben Pedahtzur: 'God has rewarded, son of redeemed by the rock.' The el (God) and tzur (rock) elements in names continue the creedal pattern.
11: liBinyamin: for Benjamin — Avidan ben Gid'oni: 'my father is judge, son of my cutter/warrior.' Benjamin's leader is the only one whose father-name references a warrior (gid'on — the same root as the judge Gideon). Benjamin later produces Israel's first king (Saul) and Paul the apostle.
12: leDan: for Dan — Achi'ezer ben Ammishaddai: 'my brother is help, son of the people of Shaddai.' Dan's leader carries a name of brotherly solidarity (ach = brother; ezer = help). Dan will later become associated with northern apostasy but here stands in covenant solidarity.
13: le-Asher: for Asher — Pag'iel ben Ocheran: 'God met/encountered me, son of the troubled.' Paga (to meet, encounter, light upon) is a verb with a range from accidental meeting to intentional encounter with God; this leader's name commemorates a divine encounter. The el suffix makes it theophoric.
14: leGad: for Gad — Elyasaf ben Re'u'el: 'God has added, son of shepherd/friend of God.' Re'u'el is the same name as Jethro's alternate name (Exodus 2:18; Numbers 10:29) — 'friend of God' (re'a el) or possibly 'shepherd of God' (ra'ah el).
15: leNaftali: for Naphtali — Achira ben Enan: 'my brother is evil/shepherd (?), son of the gracious.' Naphtali, whose territory will also be part of the Galilean region (Isaiah 9:1), is represented by a leader whose name contains ach (brother) — the fraternal solidarity motif recurring in the tribal leadership roster.
16: eleh keruyei ha'edah nesiei mattot avotam: 'these are those called of the congregation, the princes of the tribes of their fathers.' Nesiim (princes) — from nasa (lift); the nasi is the 'lifted one' — lifted above his tribe in honor and responsibility. Mattot avotam (tribes of their fathers) — each nasi represents the patriarchal lineage. The double designation — called of the congregation (keruyei ha'edah) + princes of tribes — marks their dual accountability: elected by the community, representing the ancestral line.
17: va-yikach Moshe ve-Aharon et ha-anashim ha-eleh asher nikvu beshemot: 'Moses and Aaron took these men who had been designated by name.' Nikvu beshemot (designated/pierced by names) — naqav (to specify, designate, pierce) applied to naming; to be 'named' in this context is to be officially designated. The names are not incidental; each person is individually identified in the census.
18: ve-et kol-ha'edah hikehilu be-echad lachodesh hasheni: 'they assembled all the congregation on the first of the second month.' Hakhel (convene/assemble) — from kahal (assembly, congregation); the census assembles the entire community. Vayityaldu (they declared their genealogies) — yalad (to give birth/genealogize); the census is a genealogical act, rooting each individual in family lineage.
19: ka'asher tzivah YHWH et Moshe vayifkedem bemidbar Sinai: 'as YHWH commanded Moses, he enrolled them in the wilderness of Sinai.' The census (paqad) proceeds in exact obedience to divine command. Bemidbar Sinai (in the Sinai wilderness) — the census is wilderness-situated; this is not an enrollment of settled people but of a pilgrim community en route.
20: vayihyu vnei Reuven bechor Yisrael: 'the sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel.' Reuben is enumerated first as Jacob's eldest. Each tribe's formula begins: ldorotam lemishpechotam lebeit avotam bemispar shemot lgulgelotam — 'by their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, by the number of names, head by head.' The fourfold enumeration structure runs through every tribe: generation, clan, father's house, individual name-count.
21: peqoudeihem lematteh Reuven shisha ve-arbaim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Reuben: 46,500.' Pequdim (those enrolled/numbered) from paqad. The number 46,500: elef may mean a military unit rather than a literal thousand in some usages; if so, the figure represents 46 units plus 500 individuals. The tribe numbers across the census are consistent with the total 603,550 in v46.
22: livnei Shim'on: for Simeon. The formula repeats for each tribe. Simeon's count (59,300) will decline sharply to 22,200 by the second census (Numbers 26:14) — the steepest drop of any tribe, likely connected to the Baal Peor incident (Num 25) where the leader Zimri (a Simeonite, Numbers 25:14) was killed. The census numbers are not static; they reflect covenant consequence.
23: peqoudeihem lematteh Shim'on tish'ah vachamishim elef ushlosh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Simeon: 59,300.' The paqad formula concludes each tribal count. Tish'ah vachamishim (59) — Simeon's large first-census number will make the second census decline all the more striking.
24: livnei Gad: for Gad. Gad, though sixth in Jacob's birth order (son of Zilpah), is enumerated here third after Reuben and Simeon — possibly reflecting the camp assignment (Reuben's group, south camp) rather than strict birth order. The census ordering reflects military-organizational rather than purely genealogical priorities.
25: peqoudeihem lematteh Gad chamisha vechamishim elef veshesh me'ot vachamishim: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Gad: 45,650.' Gad's count. Chamishim (fifty) — the round-number components in tribal counts have led some scholars to propose that the census counts represent administrative units, not literal population figures. The debate touches elef's dual meaning.
26: livnei Yehudah: for Judah. Judah leads the eastern camp and will be listed first in the marching order (Numbers 2:3-9, Numbers 10:14). The tribal census sequence here is Reuben, Simeon, Gad (southern camp, Numbers 2:10-16), then Judah (eastern camp). The census ordering by camp-assignment reflects the military structure of Numbers overall.
27: peqoudeihem lematteh Yehudah arba'ah veshiv'im elef veshesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Judah: 74,600' — the largest single tribe. Judah's numerical supremacy reflects the blessing of Jacob (Genesis 49:8-12) and Moses (Deuteronomy 33:7). From Judah comes the scepter that does not depart (Genesis 49:10), the royal line culminating in David and the Messiah.
28: livnei Yissachar: for Issachar. Issachar and Zebulun bracket Judah in the eastern camp (Numbers 2:5-8) — they are Judah's flanking tribes in the vanguard position. Issachar is praised in the Deborah song (Judges 5:15) and later known for wisdom ('understanding the times,' 1 Chronicles 12:32).
29: peqoudeihem lematteh Yissachar arba'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Issachar: 54,400.' Arba'ah vachamishim (fifty-four). The Issachar count is mid-range among the tribes.
30: livnei Zevulun: for Zebulun. Zebulun completes the eastern camp group with Judah and Issachar (Numbers 2:7). Zebulun is associated with the northern coastal trade region (Genesis 49:13) and with the Galilee territory where Jesus ministered (Isaiah 9:1-2 → Matthew 4:13-16).
31: peqoudeihem lematteh Zevulun shiv'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Zebulun: 57,400.' Zebulun's count makes the eastern camp total (Judah + Issachar + Zebulun): 74,600 + 54,400 + 57,400 = 186,400 — the largest camp contingent.
32: livnei Yosef livnei Efrayim: 'for the sons of Joseph, for the sons of Ephraim.' Ephraim is listed first of the two Joseph tribes, reflecting Jacob's blessing of Ephraim over Manasseh (Genesis 48:14-20: 'the younger shall be greater'). Ephraim becomes the leading tribe of the northern kingdom; the name is sometimes used as a metonym for the northern ten tribes (Isaiah 7:2; Hosea 5:3).
33: peqoudeihem lematteh Efrayim arba'ah vethlashim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Ephraim: 40,500.' Ephraim's count (40,500) is smaller than Manasseh's (32,200 is actually... wait, let me check) — actually Manasseh's count will be in v35: 32,200. Ephraim (40,500) is larger, which fits the Jacob blessing precedence. In the second census (Num 26), the numbers reverse — Manasseh becomes larger. The dynamics of blessing and census interact.
34: livnei Menashe: for Manasseh. Manasseh, though the elder of Joseph's sons, is listed after Ephraim — reflecting Jacob's cross-handed blessing. The western camp (Numbers 2:18-24) groups Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin; Ephraim leads this group.
35: peqoudeihem lematteh Menashe shnayim uthlashim elef umayatayim: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Manasseh: 32,200.' Manasseh's count is the smallest of the twelve tribes in the first census, despite being Joseph's elder son. The reversal of expected precedence (Manasseh over Ephraim → Ephraim leads) is built into the census itself. By the second census (Numbers 26:34), Manasseh will overtake Ephraim (52,700 vs. 32,500).
36: livnei Binyamin: for Benjamin. Benjamin is the youngest of the twelve sons of Jacob, born last and associated with great loss (Rachel's death, Genesis 35:18-19). Benjamin is the only tribe that will remain with Judah after the division (1 Kings 12:21). Paul identifies himself as Benjamin: 'a Hebrew of Hebrews; as to the law, a Pharisee' (Philippians 3:5).
37: peqoudeihem lematteh Binyamin chamisha uthlashim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Benjamin: 35,400.' Benjamin's count (35,400) makes the western camp total: 40,500 + 32,200 + 35,400 = 108,100 — the smallest camp contingent, which fits Benjamin's status as the youngest.
38: livnei Dan: for Dan. Dan leads the northern camp (Numbers 2:25-31), which marches last as the 'rear guard.' Dan's position at the rear — the most exposed position — is significant; Dan later becomes associated with idolatry (Judg 18; 1 Kings 12:29) and Dan's name is notably absent from the 144,000 list in Revelation 7.
39: peqoudeihem lematteh Dan shnayim veshish'im elef vesheva me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Dan: 62,700.' Dan's count is substantial. The northern camp total (Dan + Asher + Naphtali): 62,700 + 41,500 + 53,400 = 157,600.
40: livnei Asher: for Asher. Asher (son of Zilpah, Leah's maid) is flanking tribe in the northern/rear camp. The blessing of Asher (Genesis 49:20: 'Asher's food shall be rich; he shall yield royal delicacies') and Moses's blessing (Deuteronomy 33:24-25: 'most blessed of sons is Asher... as your days, so is your strength') mark him as prosperous. Anna the prophetess who greeted the infant Jesus at the temple was from Asher (Luke 2:36).
41: peqoudeihem lematteh Asher echad ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Asher: 41,500.' Asher's count.
42: livnei Naftali: for Naphtali. Naphtali (son of Bilhah, Rachel's maid) completes the rear camp group. Naphtali's territory (northern Galilee around the Sea of Galilee) is the region where Jesus conducted the majority of his ministry. Isaiah's prophecy (Numbers 9:1-2: 'the land of Zebulun and Naphtali... a great light') is cited at the beginning of Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matthew 4:13-16).
43: peqoudeihem lematteh Naftali shloshah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Naphtali: 53,400.' The formula closes the twelve-tribe census. Peqoudeihem (those enrolled/numbered): the paqad verb's participial form; each tribal entry climaxes with this enrollment count.
44: eleh hapekudim asher pakad Moshe ve-Aharon: 'these are those who were enrolled, whom Moses and Aaron enrolled.' Hapekudim... pakad: the paqad cognate construction (enrolled / who enrolled) emphasizes the divine-authorized character of the count. Venit nesiei Yisrael (and the princes of Israel with them) — the 12 tribal leaders co-authorized the census.
45: vayihyu kol-pekudei vnei-Yisrael leveit avotam mikol-anshei Yisrael: 'all those enrolled of the people of Israel, by their fathers' houses, from twenty years old and upward, all able to go to war in Israel.' The census is summarized before the total: enumerated by beit av (father's house), by age (20+), by military qualification. The threefold specification mirrors v2's counting method — the census is internally consistent.
46: vayihyu kol-hapekudim shesh-me'ot alef ushelosheth alafim wachamesh me'ot vachamishim: 'all those enrolled were 603,550.' The grand total — shesh-me'ot elef (six hundred thousand) + shelosheth alafim (three thousand) + chamesh me'ot vachamishim (550). This figure is closely tied to Exodus 12:37 ('about 600,000 men on foot') and Exodus 38:26 (603,550 in the half-shekel census). The elef debate: if some alafim are military units rather than thousands, the total becomes dramatically smaller. The scholarly debate remains unresolved; the text's own internal consistency suggests the numbers are meant as literal — an army of roughly 600,000.
47: vehaleviim lematteh avotam lo hutpakedu betocham: 'but the Levites were not enrolled among them by their tribal affiliation.' The Levite exemption from the military census is the pivot of the chapter. Lo hutpakedu (they were not enrolled/numbered) — the Levites are not paqad'd in this census. They will be numbered separately (Num 3-4) for a different purpose: not warfare but tabernacle service. The exemption distinguishes warrior-Israel from servant-Israel (the Levites).
48: ki diber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: 'for YHWH spoke to Moses, saying' — the divine instruction explaining the Levite exception. The census exception is not administrative but theological: Levites belong to a different category of service than the military tribes. The ki (because/for) signals the causal basis: it is YHWH's word, not human policy, that sets apart the Levites.
49: ach et matteh Levi lo tifkod: 'only the tribe of Levi you shall not number.' Ach (only/but) — the contrastive particle marking the single exception to the universal census. Levi is the one tribe exempt from military enrollment, set apart for tabernacle ministry. The theological point is significant: the tribe that serves at the altar stands outside the military count — a division of labor built into the covenant community's structure.
50: ve-atah haf ket et-haleviyim al mishkan ha'edut: 'but appoint the Levites over the tabernacle of the testimony.' Haf ket (appoint/set): the Levites are not exempt from service but reassigned from military to sanctuary service. Mishkan ha'edut (tabernacle of the testimony) — the edut (testimony, witness) refers to the tablets of the law in the ark; the tabernacle is defined by what it houses: the divine testimony. Levites transport, assemble, and guard the tabernacle and all its vessels.
51: veha'zar hakorev yumat: 'and the stranger [non-Levite] who draws near shall be put to death.' Zar (stranger/unauthorized person) — not a foreigner but any Israelite who is not a Levite; zar in the priestly context means 'unauthorized' (the wrong person approaching the wrong thing). This is not hostility toward outsiders but the holiness boundary: approaching the sanctuary without proper authorization is deadly because the sanctuary is YHWH's holy space. The barrier between ordinary Israel and the sanctuary is encoded in law.
52: vachanah bnei Yisrael ish al-machanehu ve'ish al-diglo letzivotam: 'the people of Israel shall camp each by his own camp and each by his own standard according to their companies.' Machaneh (camp) — the military-settlement term; the camp of Israel is an organized military encampment. Degel (standard/banner) — introduced here for the first time; the tribal grouping flag. Letzivotam (by their armies/companies) — tzava again.
53: vehaleviim yachanu saviv lemishkan ha'edut: 'but the Levites shall camp around the tabernacle of the testimony.' The spatial arrangement: tribes camp in their four-directional groups; Levites surround the tabernacle in the center. Ve-lo yihyeh ketzef (that there be no wrath) — the Levite encirclement is a protective buffer preventing unauthorized access by non-Levites. The wrath (ketzef) that would fall on Israel if non-Levites approached is deflected by the Levite cordon.
54: va-ya'asu bnei Yisrael kekol asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe ken asu: 'and the people of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses; so they did.' The closing obedience formula: ka'asher tzivah... ken asu (as commanded... so they did). Numbers 1 ends in full covenant compliance. The formula of obedience at chapter-end is a Numbers pattern that will be tested and broken in the wilderness murmurings ahead. Here, at the outset, Israel is fully obedient.
